1013	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1014	
1015	**DEFAULT: describe the solution. Write code only if asked.**
1016	
1017	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "It's in `allow`. Two requests for the SAME customer arrive at once. Both read `tokens=1`, both pass the `>= 1` check, both decrement. We granted two requests off one token — the limit is breached. Classic check-then-act."
1018	
1019	🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "One lock **per customer**. Before the read-modify-write, `allow` acquires that customer's lock; while it's held no other request for that customer can enter. Both race for the lock — one wins, the other waits, then sees the spent state and gets rejected."
1020	
1021	🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off (the key one for this problem):** "I deliberately do NOT use a single global lock. A global lock is correct but it serializes ALL customers — one customer's flood would block everyone. **Lock striping** — a `dict[customer_id -> RLock]` — means only same-customer requests contend; ten customers run in parallel. That's the headline requirement."
1022	
1023	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed (if asked):** "Across servers, an in-process lock protects only one process. I'd push atomicity into a shared store — a Redis token bucket via a Lua script (read tokens, refill, spend, write — all atomic on the Redis single thread), or a DB compare-and-set: `UPDATE state SET tokens=tokens-1 WHERE customer_id=? AND tokens>=1`; zero rows affected means you lost the race."
1024	
1025	**Concurrency Vocabulary**
1026	
1027	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1028	|---|---|---|
1029	| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | two requests for one customer in `allow` |
1030	| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the read-modify-write inside `_lock` in `allow` |
1031	| **Check-then-act** | Check a condition, then act, but state changed in between | "saw a token, then spent it" |
1032	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-customer lock guarantees |
1033	| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `RLock` per customer id |
1034	| **Reentrant (RLock)** | The same thread can re-acquire its own lock | a guarded method calling another guarded method on the same customer |
1035	| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse (global) vs fine (per-customer) | we chose per-CUSTOMER (striping) |
1036	| **Lock striping** | Many locks keyed by id instead of one global lock | the `dict[customer_id -> RLock]` |
1037	| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` |
1038	| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally and retry | the distributed DB compare-and-set |
1039	| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | Atomic "update only if value still X" | `WHERE tokens >= 1` in the distributed SQL |
1040	| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: we only ever hold ONE lock at a time |
1041	
1042	**Concurrency Follow-up Q&A**
1043	
1044	> ❓ "Why a `dict` of locks and not one global lock?"
1045	> "One global lock serializes every customer — a single abusive customer's flood would block everyone, which is exactly the requirement I must NOT violate. Striping — one `RLock` per customer id — means only same-customer requests contend; different customers take different locks and run fully in parallel. My `test_many_customers_do_not_block_each_other` proves the parallel speed-up."
1046	
1047	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1048	> "Because a guarded method might call another guarded method on the same customer. A plain `Lock` would deadlock when the same thread re-acquires it; an `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases at the outermost `with`."
1049	
1050	> ❓ "How do you protect the lock dictionary itself?"
1051	> "The map of locks needs its own lock. Two threads creating the lock for a brand-new customer at the same instant could both build one and clobber each other. So `_lock_for` get-or-creates under a tiny `_stripe_guard`, held for nanoseconds, then releases it and takes the per-customer lock — 'lock the map before you lock the thing.'"
1052	
1053	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1054	> "None by design — I only ever hold ONE lock at a time (the per-customer lock; the stripe guard is released before it's taken). No lock-ordering cycle, so no deadlock."
1055	
1056	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic?"
1057	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is microseconds and contention per customer is low, so a short-held lock beats a retry loop. For the distributed case I flip to optimistic — a Redis Lua script or a DB compare-and-set — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers."
1058	
1059	> ❓ "What if the lock dictionary grows unbounded with millions of customers?"
1060	> "The lock map would leak entries for customers who go quiet. I'd cap it with an LRU and evict idle customers' locks AND their state together — covered in the extensibility twists below."
1061	
1062	❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version:**
1063	
1064	**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
1065	```python copy
1066	# §6 clean core — the hook does nothing:
1067	@contextmanager
1068	def _lock(self, customer_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
1069	    yield
1070	
1071	# §9 thread-safe subclass — overrides ONLY the hook:
1072	class ThreadSafeRateLimiterService(RateLimiterService):
1073	    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
1074	        super().__init__(clock)
1075	        self._stripe_guard = threading.Lock()        # protects the lock map
1076	        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}  # one lock per customer
1077	
1078	    @contextmanager
1079	    def _lock(self, customer_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
1080	        lock = self._lock_for(customer_id)
1081	        with lock:
1082	            yield
1083	
1084	    def _lock_for(self, customer_id: str) -> threading.RLock:
1085	        with self._stripe_guard:               # lock the map first
1086	            lock = self._locks.get(customer_id)
1087	            if lock is None:
1088	                lock = threading.RLock()
1089	                self._locks[customer_id] = lock
1090	            return lock                         # then lock the per-customer thing
1091	```
1092	- `RLock` not `Lock` — reentrant so a guarded method can call a guarded helper on the same customer.
1093	- per-customer not global — different customers never contend.
1094	- double-lock idiom — `_stripe_guard` protects the dict; the per-customer `RLock` protects the state.
1095	
1096	**Part B — the EXACT modified method:**

1100	    policy = self._require_known(customer_id)
1101	    now = self._clock.now() if timestamp is None else timestamp
1102	    with self._lock(customer_id):                      # no-op in the core
1103	        state = self._states[customer_id]
1104	        self._guard_monotonic(customer_id, state, now)
1105	        state.last_seen = now
1106	        strategy = self._strategies[policy.algorithm]
1107	        return strategy.check(state, policy, now)

1109	# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — diff: ZERO lines in allow ───────────────────
1110	# allow() is byte-for-byte identical. The ONLY change is which _lock runs:
1111	# the subclass's _lock takes the per-customer RLock instead of yielding.
